summary: Yegge explains why he's reluctantly starting a blog despite hating the blog format. He critiques blogs for their chronological ordering, lack of threaded and inline comments, no version history, and poor content editing tools. After considering building his own solution in Ruby on Rails but realizing it would be another multi-year project like his 7-year game development effort, he pragmatically chooses Blogger. The post serves as both a manifesto for better self-publishing software and an explanation for why he's settling for imperfect tools.
key_points: Blogs should organize content by popularity, not chronology, with inline comments that grow 'outward' from specific sentences rather than 'downward' at the end
What's needed is an 'essay-publishing system' that combines blog, wiki, and social voting features with version history for posts and comments
Web programming is still a 'Big Crap Sandwich' and even Rails can't make ambitious solo projects feasible for people with day jobs
His 7-year game project taught him that ideas vastly outnumber available time, and he can't chase every 5-year idea anymore
Java's lack of dynamic development capabilities forced him to rewrite half his game in Jython - dynamic languages are essential for extensible applications
money_quotes: Blog entries should be organized by popularity, not time. Or ideally, you can pick either one. Why the hell don't blogs do that?
The commentary on a blog entry should grow outward, not downward.
Web Programming is a Big Crap Sandwich, and we all have to take a bite of it.
I've realized that I have more ideas than time. Way more. It's not even close.
If I come up with ten ideas that take 5 years each, I'm pretty screwed.
18 months, what a joke of an estimate.
It simply isn't possible to do an extensible game like that in Java. You either write your own minilanguage or you use a JVM language; those are your only choices.
I can't afford to go chasing every 5-year idea that springs to mind.
key_insight: The gap between our vision for how software should work and our ability to build it forces painful compromises, especially when ideas far outnumber available years.
